title: AUT-1997-3-008
text:
HEADNOTE:
According to the right of equal protection under the law, Austrian citizens must not be discriminated against compared with foreigners as far as there is no objective justification for doing so. A provision which requires the proof of qualifications in order to start and carry on a trade restricts the right to practise every kind of gainful activity (freedom to work for remuneration) contained in Article 6 of the Basic Constitutional Law (Staatsgrundgesetz) . Such a restriction must not be disproportionate. Exercise of a trade must also be open to those who cannot provide proof of a particular qualification but who can provide proof of equivalent training (schooling) and practice. The exclusion of persons from practising a trade merely because they have gained their training and practice in Austria and not in a foreign country (an EEA member state) is a disproportionate infringement of freedom to work for remuneration.

SUMMARY:
An Austrian citizen brought a case before the Constitutional Court complaining that his application to be exempt from the requirement of proof of qualifications to carry on trade as a travel agent had been dismissed because he had gained the required experience in Austria. The applicant argued that his rights were infringed by the impugned administrative decree because it applied a regulation contrary to the law. The Constitutional Court thus began review proceedings to ascertain whether a specific provision of the regulation applied was lawful. At the same time the Administrative Court had to examine a parallel case - an Italian citizen trained in Austria had been refused leave to practise a trade and submitted an application for the overruling of parts of the regulation applied. The Austrian Trade Law (Gewerbeordnung) stipulates that tradesmen exercising certain kinds of trade must provide proof of certain qualifications. This requirement may be waived when a person can prove they have achieved an appropriate level of qualification, either through training (course of instruction) or practical experience. The Trade Law amendment of 1992 extended the possibility of exemption from such proof for citizens of an EEA member state provided that a claim of holding the required qualification could be supported by evidence such as a certificate of relevant experience or training. Furthermore, the Minister of Economic Affairs was authorised to issue a regulation determining in more detail the conditions for such an exemption. The relevant regulation restricted this exemption to those who could prove that the required certificate or practice was gained in another EEA member state. The Constitutional Court found that this restriction, in particular by its exclusion of Austrian citizens, gave rise to a discriminatory situation which is not covered by the law authorising the regulation but exceeds its parameters. It annulled the specific part of the regulation which accordingly contradicted the Austrian Trade Law.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION:
Legal norms referred to: Articles 7 and 139 of the Constitution.
official_publication: to be published in Erkenntnisse und Beschlüsse des Verfassungsgerichtshofes (Official Digest)
